risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients were randomly assigned to either groups – MBSR group or TAU group – using computer-generated random tables." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random allocation method (computer-generated random tables), which is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as random and at low risk of selection bias.

Quote: "Patients were randomly assigned to either groups – MBSR group or TAU group – using computer-generated random tables." Comment: The report states that random assignment occurred, but it gives no explicit information about how the allocation sequence was concealed from the person enrolling participants. Computer-generated random tables describe sequence generation, not concealment, so foreknowledge of the next assignment cannot be ruled out.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The study does not report any blinding of participants or the personnel delivering the intervention. The intervention was a clearly distinguishable 8-week MBSR program versus treatment-as-usual, with additional weekly sessions only in the MBSR arm: "A total of eight weekly sessions were held for the MBSR group... Patients in the TAU group did not receive any further sessions after the health education session." Because participants and therapists would have known group assignment, and the main outcomes included self-reported anxiety, depression, and perceived stress (as well as behaviorally influenced BP/BMI), knowledge of allocation could plausibly affect outcomes. Therefore performance bias is at high risk.

Summary: The study reports that “BP of the patients was measured either by the cardiologist or a physician” and that anxiety, depression and perceived stress were assessed with self-report scales (HADS, PSS), but it does not state whether the cardiologist/physician or any other outcome assessor was blinded to group assignment. Comment: Because the outcomes are mixed—subjective patient-reported measures (highly vulnerable to detection bias if the patient knew allocation) plus blood pressure measured by clinicians—and blinding of outcome assessment is not described, the risk of bias for this domain is unclear.

Quote: "Of the 30 patients, there were a total of 17 available at 3-month follow-up (12 in the MBSR and five in the TAU group)." Comment: Attrition was substantial overall (13/30, 43%) and clearly imbalanced at follow-up, with far fewer TAU participants available than MBSR participants. Although the authors report that baseline scores of those available versus unavailable for follow-up did not differ significantly, the large and unequal loss to follow-up could plausibly bias the follow-up results and was not handled with a clearly robust missing-data method.
